label: steelman-poles
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: identify the poles only. State (1) the conclusion most California
public-agency lawyers would reach here, and (2) the opposing position that
deserves construction. State the disfavored position neutrally and WITHOUT
supporting reasoning — someone else will build the case and must not be
anchored by yours.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

Identify the conventional view and the disfavored position.

conventional_view.description: the conclusion most California public-agency lawyers would reach, in 1-3 sentences

disfavored_position.description: the opposing position that deserves serious construction, stated neutrally in 1-3 sentences, WITHOUT any supporting reasoning

text: {"conventional_view":"Most CA public-agency lawyers would conclude the Signal messages are \"public records\" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 because their content — franchise terms, diversion targets, what council would \"realistically accept,\" RFP timing — relates to the conduct of public business, regardless of the personal-device/personal-account medium. The clerk's search of only city systems was inadequate; City of San Jose obligates the agency to make reasonable, good-faith inquiry of individual officials reasonably believed to hold responsive records on personal accounts, so the \"no responsive records\" response likely violated the Act, exposing the city to declaratory/injunctive relief and CPRA fee-shifting (Gov. Code § 7913). The auto-deletion doesn't itself violate CPRA (no CPRA retention mandate or damages remedy), but the records were arguably subject to the § 34090 four-year franchise-correspondence schedule once created, so their loss before that period lapsed raises a separate retention-duty problem with weak enforcement mechanisms (possible § 6200 referral); the surviving 40 screenshots must now be preserved and produced. Separately, three of five councilmembers deliberating collectively on franchise terms outside a noticed meeting likely creates Brown Act exposure (Gov. Code § 54952.2) that compounds — and lends credibility to — the CPRA claim, and the city should now issue a litigation hold, interview/search officials' personal devices, produce the screenshots, and adopt a policy barring ephemeral/disappearing-message apps for city business going forward.","disfavored_position":"The Signal messages are not city public records because the group was created and controlled by a private consultant on a third-party platform never touching city systems, so they were not \"prepared, owned, used, or retained\" by the agency, and informal participation by a subset of officials in a privately-run chat does not convert it into an official communication channel or agency record. The clerk's search of city systems satisfied the CPRA's \"reasonable effort\" standard absent any actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of the request, City of San Jose does not require agencies to affirmatively interrogate officials about undisclosed personal-device communications without a triggering fact, any § 34090 retention duty for such records (if it exists at all) would run to the individual councilmembers rather than the city since the city never possessed them, and with no bad-faith search shown and no CPRA damages provision, remedies should be limited to prospective declaratory/injunctive relief; the Brown Act claim is likewise a distinct cause of action requiring proof of collective concurrence toward a decision, which informal information-sharing in a bidder-run chat does not necessarily establish."}

data.conventional_view: Most CA public-agency lawyers would conclude the Signal messages are "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 because their content — franchise terms, diversion targets, what council would "realistically accept," RFP timing — relates to the conduct of public business, regardless of the personal-device/personal-account medium. The clerk's search of only city systems was inadequate; City of San Jose obligates the agency to make reasonable, good-faith inquiry of individual officials reasonably believed to hold responsive records on personal accounts, so the "no responsive records" response likely violated the Act, exposing the city to declaratory/injunctive relief and CPRA fee-shifting (Gov. Code § 7913). The auto-deletion doesn't itself violate CPRA (no CPRA retention mandate or damages remedy), but the records were arguably subject to the § 34090 four-year franchise-correspondence schedule once created, so their loss before that period lapsed raises a separate retention-duty problem with weak enforcement mechanisms (possible § 6200 referral); the surviving 40 screenshots must now be preserved and produced. Separately, three of five councilmembers deliberating collectively on franchise terms outside a noticed meeting likely creates Brown Act exposure (Gov. Code § 54952.2) that compounds — and lends credibility to — the CPRA claim, and the city should now issue a litigation hold, interview/search officials' personal devices, produce the screenshots, and adopt a policy barring ephemeral/disappearing-message apps for city business going forward.
data.disfavored_position: The Signal messages are not city public records because the group was created and controlled by a private consultant on a third-party platform never touching city systems, so they were not "prepared, owned, used, or retained" by the agency, and informal participation by a subset of officials in a privately-run chat does not convert it into an official communication channel or agency record. The clerk's search of city systems satisfied the CPRA's "reasonable effort" standard absent any actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of the request, City of San Jose does not require agencies to affirmatively interrogate officials about undisclosed personal-device communications without a triggering fact, any § 34090 retention duty for such records (if it exists at all) would run to the individual councilmembers rather than the city since the city never possessed them, and with no bad-faith search shown and no CPRA damages provision, remedies should be limited to prospective declaratory/injunctive relief; the Brown Act claim is likewise a distinct cause of action requiring proof of collective concurrence toward a decision, which informal information-sharing in a bidder-run chat does not necessarily establish.
